proof_stages.label: Index-zero embedded-curve problem and stable-map artifacts
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up moduli spaces of embedded images of J-holomorphic curves in a symplectic six-manifold, specializes to Calabi-Yau classes with expected dimension zero, and explains why transversality, noncompactness, and ghost stable maps obstruct naive geometric counting.

proof_stages.label: Main theorem: finiteness and primitive invariance
proof_stages.summary: After positioning the desired count against Taubes-Gromov and Donaldson-Thomas-type theories, the speaker states the joint theorem: for generic J there are only finitely many embedded curves in a Calabi-Yau class across all genera, and for primitive classes their signed genus-g count is a J-independent integer vanishing for large genus, with remarks on super-rigidity, positive-index classes, and current compactness.

proof_stages.label: Gopakumar-Vafa interpretation and primitive finiteness corollary
proof_stages.summary: The Gopakumar-Vafa/BPS formula is reviewed, the integrality and large-genus vanishing conjectures are separated, and Zinger's identification of primitive BPS numbers with the naive embedded-curve counts is combined with the theorem to obtain large-genus vanishing for primitive classes.

proof_stages.label: Primitive compactification and bubble pruning
proof_stages.summary: For primitive classes, multiple covers are absent, so the proof of invariance studies the closure in stable maps and uses six-dimensional index and intersection arguments to rule out most nonconstant bubbling, leaving only ghost-bubble degenerations.

proof_stages.label: Ghost-bubble gluing obstruction
proof_stages.summary: A Kuranishi/gluing model near a ghost component gives a leading obstruction term involving a holomorphic one-form and the bubble derivative, showing that a smoothable ghost degeneration must attach at a singular/cuspidal point, which is generically excluded in zero- and one-parameter families and hence gives compactness and invariance of the primitive count.

proof_stages.label: All-genus finiteness via current limits and normal rescaling
proof_stages.summary: Assuming infinitely many embedded curves in one homology class, the proof takes a current limit, uses regularity to identify it with an embedded J-holomorphic curve, rescales in its normal bundle to obtain a second holomorphic current limit, and extracts a nonzero holomorphic normal vector field contradicting generic rigidity.

proof_stages.label: Nonprimitive multiple covers and weighted-count outlook
proof_stages.summary: The normal-rescaling picture is reinterpreted for branched or multivalued normal graphs, connecting the nonprimitive case to Wendl-type super-rigidity and motivating a conjectural weighted count of embedded curves that could provide a symplectic analogue of Donaldson-Thomas invariants.

examples.name: Clemens conjecture for generic quintic threefolds
examples.note: The conjecture is cited only to illustrate that even finiteness and transversality for rational curves in a Calabi-Yau threefold are highly nontrivial in algebraic geometry.
examples.engagement_reason: the Clemens conjecture is stated as an illustrative hard algebraic-geometry finiteness problem, with no calculation or verification on a quintic

examples.name: Single primitive rational curve in the Gopakumar-Vafa formula
examples.note: The hypothetical isolated primitive rational curve demonstrates how one geometric curve can contribute to infinitely many Gromov-Witten invariants through the GV formula, after the formula has already been stated.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the Gopakumar-Vafa formula to the hypothetical single primitive rational curve and traces its contributions to higher classes and genera
